content: read reviews/62.md. if the summary section of the checklist review indicates the review passed, do nothing. if it failed, make relevant updates that are needed to deepmind/62.lean accordingly. if in doubt, reference CHECKLIST.md, which was the source document used when a previous instance of claude produced reviews/62.md.

    45→
    46→### `answer()` usage
    47→
    48→The original problem asks: *"If G₁, G₂ are two graphs with chromatic number ℵ₁ then must there exist a graph G whose chromatic number is 4 (or even ℵ₀) which is a subgraph of both?"* — this is a **yes/no question** ("must there exist"), so **Case 1** applies.

    50→- [x] Both theorems use `answer(sorry) ↔ P` form (Case 1, yes/no).
    51→- [x] Problem is open, so `answer(sorry)` is correct (not `True`/`False`).
    52→- [x] RHS formalizes the question as asked. Strong version: common subgraph with `chromaticNumber = ⊤` (infinite). Weak version: common subgraph with `¬ H.Colorable 3` (chromatic number ≥ 4). Both align with the original problem's "chromatic number 4 (or even ℵ₀)".
    53→- [x] Quantifiers go **after** `answer(sorry)`: all `∀` quantifiers are on the RHS of `↔`.

    66→- [x] No duplicate numbers.
    67→- [x] `5` is a valid AMS MSC2020 code (Combinatorics — appropriate for graph chromatic number problems).

file.content:
# Review: deepmind/62.lean

## 1. Copyright Header

- [x] File begins with the **exact** copyright block (no leading blank line).
- [x] Year is exactly `2026`.

## 2. Import Statement

- [x] Exactly one import line, immediately after the copyright block (separated by one blank line): `import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports` on line 17.
- [x] No other imports are present.

## 3. Module Docstring

- [x] A `/-! ... -/` module docstring appears after the import, separated by one blank line (line 19).
- [x] Starts with a heading: `# Erdős Problem 62`.
- [x] Contains a `*Reference:*` line with a markdown link: `[erdosproblems.com/62](https://www.erdosproblems.com/62)`.
- [x] Includes a brief plain-English description of the problem (lines 24–25).
- [x] Bibliography entries present: `[EHS74]` (Erdős–Hajnal–Shelah, 1974) and `[Er87]` (Erdős, 1987).

## 4. Namespace

- [x] All declarations are inside `namespace Erdos62 ... end Erdos62` (lines 36–92).
- [x] Namespace name uses `UpperCamelCase`: `Erdos62`.
- [x] `open SimpleGraph Cardinal` appears **before** the namespace block (line 34).

## 5. Custom Definitions

- [x] `HasChromaticNumberAleph1` (line 40) has a LaTeX docstring (lines 38–39).
- [x] `ContainsSubgraph` (line 45) has a LaTeX docstring (line 44).
- [x] No `noncomputable` needed — both definitions return `Prop`, so no classical choice issues.
- [x] No `sorry` in definitions.
- [x] Props use `UpperCamelCase`: `HasChromaticNumberAleph1`, `ContainsSubgraph`.
- [x] No problem-specific `local notation` needed.
- [ ] No `@[category test]` or `@[category API]` sanity checks for the custom definitions. *(Optional — the checklist says "consider adding".)*

## 6. Theorem Statements

- [x] Both use the `theorem` keyword.
- [x] Main theorem named `erdos_62` (snake_case, matching namespace number).
- [x] Names follow `snake_case` convention: `erdos_62`, `erdos_62.variants.weak`.
- [x] Each theorem has `@[category ...]` and `@[AMS ...]` on the **same attribute line**: `@[category research open, AMS 5]`.
- [x] Both theorems have docstrings.
- [x] Docstrings reference source papers: `[Er87]`, `[EHS74]`.

### `answer()` usage

The original problem asks: *"If G₁, G₂ are two graphs with chromatic number ℵ₁ then must there exist a graph G whose chromatic number is 4 (or even ℵ₀) which is a subgraph of both?"* — this is a **yes/no question** ("must there exist"), so **Case 1** applies.

- [x] Both theorems use `answer(sorry) ↔ P` form (Case 1, yes/no).
- [x] Problem is open, so `answer(sorry)` is correct (not `True`/`False`).
- [x] RHS formalizes the question as asked. Strong version: common subgraph with `chromaticNumber = ⊤` (infinite). Weak version: common subgraph with `¬ H.Colorable 3` (chromatic number ≥ 4). Both align with the original problem's "chromatic number 4 (or even ℵ₀)".
- [x] Quantifiers go **after** `answer(sorry)`: all `∀` quantifiers are on the RHS of `↔`.

## 7. Category Attribute

- [x] Every theorem has exactly one `@[category ...]`.
- [x] Value is `research open` — valid.
- [x] Both `research open` theorems contain `sorry` in their proof bodies.

## 8. AMS Attribute

- [x] Every theorem has exactly one `@[AMS ...]` attribute.
- [x] At least one AMS subject number listed: `5`.
- [x] Numbers in ascending order (trivially — only one).
- [x] No duplicate numbers.
- [x] `5` is a valid AMS MSC2020 code (Combinatorics — appropriate for graph chromatic number problems).

## 9. Proof Bodies

- [x] Both theorems use the multi-line `by\n  sorry` form, which is acceptable.
- [x] No `sorry` in `def` or `abbrev` declarations.
- [x] No informal proof sketches for these open research problems.
- [x] No `native_decide`.

## 10. Variants & Multi-part Problems

- [x] Variant named `erdos_62.variants.weak` — follows `erdos_N.variants.<descriptor>` pattern.
- [x] Variant lives in the **same file** as the main theorem.
- [x] Variant has its own `@[category research open, AMS 5]` attributes.
- [x] Variant has a docstring explaining the weakening (chromatic number ≥ 4 instead of infinite) and its relationship to [EHS74] and [Er87].

## 11. Code Quality

- [x] 2-space indentation throughout.
- [x] Unicode math symbols used: `∀`, `∃`, `→`, `↔`, `¬`, `ℕ`.
- [x] No debug code (`#check`, `#eval`, `#print`, `dbg_trace`).
- [x] No `set_option` disabling linters.
- [x] Lines are reasonably short (all under 100 characters).
- [x] File ends with a single trailing newline.

## 12. Build Verification

- [ ] `lake build` not run during this review. *(Must be verified before merge.)*
- [ ] Custom linter pass not verified. *(Must be verified before merge.)*

---

## Summary

**Verdict: PASS** (pending build verification)

The file is clean and well-structured. Both the strong and weak variants of the conjecture are correctly formalized as yes/no questions using `answer(sorry) ↔ P`. The custom definitions `HasChromaticNumberAleph1` and `ContainsSubgraph` are well-documented and mathematically appropriate. The split into strong (infinite chromatic number) and weak (chromatic number ≥ 4) variants faithfully reflects the original problem's phrasing. No checklist violations found.

content.text: The review verdict is **PASS**. No changes needed.
